title: Magnetism: A First-Principles View
description: From moving charges to magnets — a unified explanation.

sections.title: The Origin of Magnetism
sections.content: Magnetism arises when electric charges move. A stationary charge produces only an electric field, but once it moves, relativistic effects cause additional forces that we describe as magnetism. This means magnetism is not separate from electricity — it is a consequence of motion.

sections.title: Magnetic Fields Are Not Objects
sections.content: Field lines are just a visual tool. A magnetic field is a rule: it tells you what force a moving charge will experience. The key equation is F = q(v × B), meaning the force depends on velocity and field direction.

sections.title: Current in a Wire
sections.content: A current is simply moving charge. This creates a circular magnetic field around the wire. The strength decreases with distance and increases with current.

sections.title: Solenoids and Field Superposition
sections.content: A solenoid is many loops of wire. Each loop produces a field, and together they combine to form a strong, uniform field inside. This demonstrates that fields add together — they are not isolated effects.

sections.title: Bar Magnets and Domains
sections.content: A bar magnet is made of tiny atomic current loops. When these align, their fields combine to create a large-scale magnetic field. Without alignment, no net magnetism appears.

sections.title: Forces on Moving Charges
sections.content: When a charge moves through a magnetic field, it experiences a force perpendicular to its motion. This causes circular or curved motion — the basis of many real-world technologies.
